era_context: The Roman Tetrarchy is fragmenting rapidly. Constantine I controls the Western provinces; Maxentius rules Italy and North Africa; Licinius and Maximinus Daia contest the East. The Great Persecution of Christians continues. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is in terminal decline following the War of the Eight Princes; warlordism and non-Han migration are accelerating. In Persia, Shapur II has recently ascended to the throne. This is a year of acute political instability across multiple empires.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Silk Road dynamics

events.title: Emperor Huai of Jin Ascends to Throne; Western Jin Dynasty Continues Decline
events.description: Emperor Huai of Jin (r. 307–313 CE) ascends to the throne following the death of Emperor Hui of Jin (r. 290–306 CE). Huai's reign marks the final phase of the Western Jin Dynasty (266–316 CE). The central government's authority is nominal; regional warlords exercise de facto independence. Large-scale migration of non-Han peoples (Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, Qiang) into North China accelerates. The South remains nominally under Jin control but is increasingly autonomous. This period sees widespread famine, warfare, and population displacement. Emperor Huai's reign will end with the Jin court's flight to the South and the establishment of the Eastern Jin Dynasty (317–420 CE).
key_figures: Emperor Hui of Jin (predecessor, r. 290–306 CE)

sources.name: Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled 7th century CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century CE

events.certainty_note: Emperor Huai's accession in 307 CE is attested in Chinese historical sources. The date is secure, though details of his reign are compiled centuries later.
cross_references: 290-001 (Emperor Hui of Jin ascends to throne)

events.title: Licinius Consolidates Control of Eastern Roman Empire
events.description: Licinius (r. 308–324 CE) consolidates control of the Eastern Roman Empire following the Conference of Carnuntum (308 CE). He is recognized as the legitimate Eastern emperor by Galerius and the Tetrarchic system, though his authority is contested by Maximinus Daia in the Eastern provinces. Licinius rules from Nicomedia and later from Sirmium. His reign is marked by conflict with Maximinus Daia and later with Constantine I. By 307 CE, Licinius is establishing himself as a major power in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Roman coins and inscriptions from Licinius's reign
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43

events.certainty_note: Licinius's consolidation of Eastern power is well-attested in numismatic evidence and historical sources. The date and extent of his control by 307 CE are secure.

events.title: Maximinus Daia Contests Eastern Roman Authority; Persecution of Christians Continues
events.description: Maximinus Daia (r. 305–313 CE), Caesar of the Eastern provinces, contests the authority of Licinius and Galerius. Maximinus controls Egypt, Syria, and parts of Asia Minor, and is attempting to expand his power. The Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE) is particularly severe under Maximinus, who uses religious persecution as a tool of political control. Maximinus's conflict with Licinius will eventually lead to civil war in the Eastern provinces (312–313 CE). By 307 CE, Maximinus is establishing himself as a major contender for Eastern imperial power.
key_figures: Maximinus Daia (Caesar and later Augustus, r. 305–313 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church, Book 8, chapters 16–17
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 33–35

events.certainty_note: Maximinus Daia's rule and persecution of Christians are well-attested in multiple sources. The date and extent of his power by 307 CE are secure.
cross_references: 312-001 (Civil war between Licinius and Maximinus Daia)

events.title: Constantine I Marries Fausta; Alliance with Maximian
events.description: Constantine I marries Fausta, the daughter of the retired emperor Maximian. This marriage is intended to strengthen Constantine's position and create an alliance with Maximian against other claimants to imperial power. However, the marriage also creates complications; Maximian later attempts to reassert power and comes into conflict with Constantine. The marriage produces several children, including Constantine II, Constantius II, and Constans, who will all become emperors. By 307 CE, Constantine is consolidating his power through both military strength and dynastic alliances.
key_figures: Fausta (daughter of Maximian, wife of Constantine)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43

events.certainty_note: Constantine's marriage to Fausta is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Sources place it between 307 and 312 CE. The date 307 CE is conventional but not definitively confirmed.

events.title: Galerius Remains Senior Emperor; Health Deteriorates
events.description: Galerius continues as the senior emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire, ruling from Nicomedia. However, his health is reportedly deteriorating; sources describe a wasting illness (possibly gout or a chronic disease) that will eventually lead to his death in 311 CE. Galerius's illness and declining health contribute to the instability of the Eastern provinces and the rise of Licinius and Maximinus Daia as competing powers. By 307 CE, Galerius's authority is increasingly nominal, and the Eastern provinces are fragmenting into competing spheres of influence.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church, Book 8, chapters 16–17
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 33–35

events.certainty_note: Galerius's health problems are attested in multiple sources, but the specific onset and progression of his illness are difficult to date precisely. Sources are written decades later and may exaggerate or minimize his health problems.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Constantine's marriage to Fausta as a purely political alliance is somewhat anachronistic; while dynastic marriages were politically motivated, they also had personal and emotional dimensions that are difficult to assess from surviving sources. The extent of Maximinus Daia's persecution of Christians in 307 CE is difficult to assess; sources written decades later may exaggerate the severity. The portrayal of Galerius as a persecutor of Christians who is divinely punished is partly a Christian apologetic narrative; Galerius's illness may have had natural causes unrelated to his religious policies.
historiographic_note: The year 307 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through numismatic evidence and near-contemporary chronicles, though sources carry Christian apologetic bias. For China, sources are detailed but compiled centuries later. For Sassanid Persia, evidence is primarily numismatic and from much later Persian chronicles. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian elites.

graph_edges.note: Emperor Huai's reign led to the final collapse of Western Jin
Licinius's consolidation of Eastern power led to his eventual conflict with Constantine
Maximinus Daia's persecution and conflict with Licinius led to the Edict of Milan
Constantine's marriage to Fausta strengthened his position for the Milvian Bridge campaign
